questions.question: Explain the MapReduce programming model with its three main phases. Include the role of each phase.
keyPoints: Map Phase: Processes input data splits in parallel, transforms records into intermediate key-value pairs, runs independently without communication between mappers.
Shuffle and Sort Phase: Partitions mapper outputs using hash function, transfers data to appropriate reducers, groups all values for same key in sorted order.
Reduce Phase: Receives grouped key-value pairs, aggregates/processes values for each key, produces final output written to HDFS.
Optional Combiner: Local reducer on mapper node for preliminary aggregation, reduces network transfer, only for associative/commutative operations.

questions.question: Describe how MapReduce can be used for IoT batch analytics. Provide a detailed example of calculating daily average temperature from sensor data.
keyPoints: IoT Use Case: Smart building with thousands of temperature sensors generating readings every minute, need daily average per sensor.
Input Data Format: CSV records with date, time, sensor_id, and temperature value.
Map Function: Parse each record, extract date and sensor_id as composite key, emit (date-sensor_id, temperature) pairs.
Shuffle and Sort: Groups all temperature readings for same date-sensor combination.
Reduce Function: For each key, iterate through temperature list, calculate sum and count, compute average = sum/count.
Optimization: Use Combiner to calculate local sum and count on mapper nodes to reduce network traffic.
Benefits: Parallel processing of petabytes of historical data, fault tolerance, scalable to millions of sensors.

questions.question: Explain the role of the Combiner and Partitioner in MapReduce with examples.
keyPoints: Combiner: Local reducer on mapper node that reduces data transferred during shuffle. Works only for associative/commutative operations (sum, count, max, min).
Example: In word count, combiner locally sums word counts before shuffle, reducing network traffic by orders of magnitude.
Partitioner: Determines which reducer receives each key-value pair. Default HashPartitioner uses hash(key) mod num_reducers.
Custom partitioners used when data is skewed or specific routing is needed (e.g., range partitioning for sorted output).

questions.question: Discuss how MapReduce achieves fault tolerance. Explain data locality optimization and speculative execution with examples.
keyPoints: Fault tolerance mechanisms: Automatic task retry (default 4 attempts), re-execution on different nodes if needed, input data replicated across HDFS.
Speculative execution: Detects slow (straggler) tasks, launches duplicate copies on idle nodes, first to finish wins, other killed.
Data locality optimization: Scheduler tries to assign tasks to nodes where data resides (node-local > rack-local > off-rack).
Node-local: Task on same node as data block, zero network transfer, highest performance.
Rack-local: Task on same rack, minimal cross-rack traffic. Off-rack: Task on different rack, full network transfer.
IoT example: Processing 10TB of sensor data, MapReduce schedules 1000 mappers near their respective HDFS blocks, automatically retries if any node fails, speculative execution handles slow nodes.
